records.ety: [F. dmettre, p. p. dmis, dmise, to put away, lay down; pref. d- (L. de or dis-) + mettre to put, place, lay, fr. L. mittere to send. See Mission, and cf. Dismiss, Demit.]

defs: Transmission by formal act or conveyance to an heir or successor; transference; especially, the transfer or transmission of the crown or royal authority to a successor.
The decease of a royal or princely person; hence, also, the death of any illustrious person.
quotes: After the demise of the Queen [of George II.], in 1737, they [drawing- rooms] were held but twice a week.

defs: The conveyance or transfer of an estate, either in fee for life or for years, most commonly the latter.
notes: ☞ The demise of the crown is a transfer of the crown, royal authority, or kingdom, to a successor. Thus, when Edward IV. was driven from his throne for a few months by the house of Lancaster, this temporary transfer of his dignity was called a demise. Thus the natural death of a king or queen came to be denominated a demise, as by that event the crown is transferred to a successor.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Demised (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Demising.]

defs: To transfer or transmit by succession or inheritance; to grant or bestow by will; to bequeath.
quotes: What honor Canst thou demise to any child of mine?
His soul is at his conception demised to him.

defs: To convey, as an estate, by lease; to lease.
